attrs.alt: Computer Use & GUI Control — concept overview

content.text: Computer Use: Agents Controlling the Desktop

Computer use lets an AI model control a computer the same way a human does: looking at the screen via screenshots, moving the mouse, clicking, typing, pressing keyboard shortcuts, and reading what appeared. Unlike browser automation (which requires a DOM) or API calls (which require an endpoint), computer use works on any application — legacy desktop software, visual dashboards, applications with no API, and operating system interfaces.

Anthropic's Claude models support computer use through a dedicated set of tools in the Messages API. The model receives a screenshot and emits actions such as click at coordinate X,Y, type text, or press a key. The host machine executes the actions, takes a new screenshot, and sends it back to the model. This
continues until the task is complete.
Screenshot, mouse move, left/right/middle click, double click, drag, scroll, type, key press
Execute a shell command and return stdout/stderr
View, create, and edit files with string replacement
To enable computer use, pass the built-in tool definitions to the Messages API. The
matching the actual screen resolution. After every action, a new screenshot is sent back as the tool result so the model sees the updated screen state.
is a versioned built-in type recognized by the Anthropic API. The model emits
field for mouse actions. After every action, capture a new screenshot and send it as the tool result — the model needs to see the updated screen state to decide its next step.
in PyAutoGUI lets you abort runaway automation by moving the mouse to any screen corner.
provides local GUI automation. It works on macOS, Windows, and Linux with a display. Use
to find UI elements by reference image rather than hard-coded coordinates — more reliable across resolutions.

# pip install pyautogui Pillow
import pyautogui, time
from PIL import Image

pyautogui.PAUSE = 0.5        # 0.5s pause between actions
pyautogui.FAILSAFE = True    # move to screen corner to abort

def take_screenshot_pil() -> Image.Image:
    """Take a screenshot and return a PIL Image."""
    return pyautogui.screenshot()

def click_image(reference_png_path: str, confidence: float = 0.8) -> bool:
    """
    Find a reference image on screen and click its center.
    Returns True if found and clicked.
    """
    location = pyautogui.locateOnScreen(reference_png_path, confidence=confidence)
    if location is None:
        return False
    center = pyautogui.center(location)
    pyautogui.click(center)
    return True

def type_into_field(label_png_path: str, text: str) -> bool:
    """
    Click a field identified by a label image snippet, then type text.
    label_png_path: path to a small PNG of the field label.
    """
    location = pyautogui.locateOnScreen(label_png_path, confidence=0.8)
    if location is None:
        return False
    # Click to the right of the label (where the input box likely is)
    center = pyautogui.center(location)
    pyautogui.click(center.x + 150, center.y)
    time.sleep(0.2)
    pyautogui.hotkey("ctrl", "a")  # select all existing text
    pyautogui.typewrite(text, interval=0.05)
    return True

# Example: open a URL in the default browser
pyautogui.hotkey("ctrl", "l")        # focus address bar
time.sleep(0.3)
pyautogui.typewrite("https://example.com", interval=0.05)
pyautogui.press("enter")
time.sleep(2.0)                      # wait for page load
screenshot = pyautogui.screenshot()
screenshot.save("/tmp/after_nav.png")

uses template matching to find a small reference image within the current screenshot. This is more reliable than hard-coded (x, y) coordinates because it adapts to different window positions and screen resolutions.

adds a half-second pause between actions — applications need time to render before the next action. For agent use, pair PyAutoGUI actions with a vision model: take a screenshot, send it to Claude or GPT-4o to identify what element to interact with next, then call the appropriate PyAutoGUI function.
Running in an Isolated Virtual Display
never run on your primary machine

: the agent can click anything, open files, and take irreversible actions. On Linux, use Xvfb (X Virtual Framebuffer) to create a headless display. All GUI actions go to the virtual screen, invisible to the real monitor.

# Install: apt install xvfb scrot
import subprocess, os, time

def start_virtual_display(
    display_num: int = 99, width: int = 1280, height: int = 800
) -> subprocess.Popen:
    """Start a virtual X display and set the DISPLAY env var."""
    proc = subprocess.Popen(
        ["Xvfb", f":{display_num}", "-screen", "0", f"{width}x{height}x24"],
        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,
    )
    time.sleep(0.5)  # give Xvfb time to start
    os.environ["DISPLAY"] = f":{display_num}"
    return proc

def take_screenshot_xvfb(display_num: int = 99) -> str:
    """Capture the virtual display and return base64-encoded PNG."""
    import base64
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["scrot", f"-d", str(display_num), "-"],
        capture_output=True,
        timeout=5,
        env={**os.environ, "DISPLAY": f":{display_num}"},
    )
    return base64.standard_b64encode(result.stdout).decode()

# Run an agent on the virtual display
vdisplay = start_virtual_display()
try:
    # Open a browser on the virtual display
    subprocess.Popen(
        ["chromium", "--no-sandbox", "https://example.com"],
        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,
    )
    time.sleep(3.0)  # wait for page load
    screenshot_b64 = take_screenshot_xvfb()
    print(f"Captured screenshot: {len(screenshot_b64)} bytes (base64)")
    # Pass screenshot_b64 to the computer use agent loop
finally:
    vdisplay.terminate()
environment variable directs all X11 applications (browsers, text editors, desktop apps) to the virtual display. A real monitor never shows what happens inside the virtual display. Use

to capture the virtual display to stdout. For cloud deployments, this approach works in headless servers with no GPU. The virtual display can be any resolution — use 1280x800 or larger to give the model enough screen real estate to see UI elements clearly.
Computer use agents have the same capabilities as the logged-in user. Apply these constraints before deploying:
: run in a dedicated VM, Docker container, or virtual display. Never use a machine logged into sensitive accounts.
: reset the environment after each agent session — delete downloaded files, close browsers, clear clipboard.
: clicking 'Confirm payment', 'Send email', or 'Delete files' should pause and show the action to a human before executing.
: limit agent runs to 20–50 steps. Runaway agents that click in loops can cause serious damage before anyone notices.
: log every action the agent takes (screenshot + action description) to a timestamped file for post-hoc review.
Rather than having the model guess coordinates,

sends each screenshot to a vision model that identifies the relevant UI element and returns its screen coordinates. This separates the task of recognizing UI from the task of reasoning about what to do — each model plays to its strength.

import anthropic, base64, json, re
from PIL import Image
import io

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def get_coordinates_from_vision(screenshot_b64: str, element_description: str) -> tuple[int, int] | None:
    """
    Ask a vision model to identify the coordinates of a UI element.
    Returns (x, y) or None if not found.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": [
                    {
                        "type": "image",
                        "source": {
                            "type": "base64",
                            "media_type": "image/png",
                            "data": screenshot_b64,
                        },
                    },
                    {
                        "type": "text",
                        "text": (
                            f"Find the {element_description} in this screenshot. "
                            "Reply ONLY with: FOUND: x=<number> y=<number> "
                            "or NOT_FOUND if you cannot locate it."
                        ),
                    },
                ],
            }
        ],
    )
    text = response.content[0].text.strip()
    match = re.search(r"x=(\d+)\s+y=(\d+)", text)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1)), int(match.group(2))
    return None

def smart_click(screenshot_b64: str, element_description: str) -> str:
    """
    Ask vision model for coordinates, then click that element.
    Returns status message.
    """
    coords = get_coordinates_from_vision(screenshot_b64, element_description)
    if coords is None:
        return f"Could not find: {element_description}"
    x, y = coords
    import pyautogui
    pyautogui.click(x, y)
    return f"Clicked '{element_description}' at ({x}, {y})"

# Usage in an agent loop:
# screenshot_b64 = take_screenshot()
# result = smart_click(screenshot_b64, "the Search button")
# print(result)  # Clicked 'the Search button' at (640, 120)
The vision model acts as a
: it reads the screenshot and returns coordinates in a structured format. The regex
extracts the numbers from the model's response. A faster model (
) is appropriate here since coordinate identification is a simple visual task. The reasoning agent calls
with a natural language description like 'the Submit button' or 'the search input field' — no hard-coded coordinates anywhere.

: instead of using the GUI to open a terminal or edit a file, the agent can execute shell commands and edit files directly. This is faster and more reliable than navigating the desktop for file-system tasks.
tool lets the agent run shell commands directly — listing files, checking processes, running scripts, or installing packages. The

) handles file reads, creates, and targeted string replacements. String replacement is preferable to rewriting an entire file: the model only needs to specify what changed, not the full file content. Both tools complement computer use: use
for visual navigation of applications that have no CLI.
Computer use is the most general agent integration: any application visible on screen becomes controllable.

Anthropic's computer use API lets Claude take screenshot, click, type, and key actions in a structured loop. After every action, a new screenshot is sent back so the model sees the result. PyAutoGUI provides the same capability for local scripting with locateOnScreen for reliable UI targeting. Always run computer use agents in an isolated virtual display or VM, cap the number of steps, and require human approval for irreversible actions like purchases and deletions.
